WhatsApp Receptionist Decision Doc

Source reference
- project-brain/WhatsApp-Based Receptionist System for Barber Scheduling.pdf

Chosen stack
- AI strategy: Option 4
- Booking logic: deterministic workflow first
- LLM role: optional helper layer later, never the source of booking truth
- Scheduling system of record: custom scheduling engine on Postgres
- WhatsApp integration: WhatsApp Business Platform (Cloud API)

Why this is the chosen direction
- We want booking accuracy and predictable behavior.
- We want full control over barber-specific scheduling rules.
- We want scheduling to be product IP, not vendor logic.
- We want lower long-term messaging cost than Twilio.
- We still want room to add AI later for better UX.

Official product rules
- The backend decides bookings, reschedules, cancellations, reminders, and conflicts.
- The database is the source of truth.
- The LLM, if added later, can help interpret messages but cannot decide business rules.
- WhatsApp is the conversation channel, not the scheduling engine.

Chosen AI approach
- Phase 1:
  - deterministic conversation state machine
  - fixed states for booking, reschedule, cancellation, reminders
  - no LLM in the critical path
- Phase 2:
  - small LLM for intent classification
  - FAQ answers
  - parsing messy free-text messages
  - tone polishing for replies

What a deterministic conversation state machine means
- The conversation moves through explicit states such as:
  - start
  - choose_service
  - choose_barber
  - choose_date
  - choose_time
  - confirm_booking
  - done
- Each state accepts only valid next inputs.
- The same input in the same state always produces the same result.
- This makes the system predictable, testable, and safer for appointment operations.

Chosen scheduling architecture
- Postgres stores:
  - appointments
  - barbers
  - services
  - working hours
  - buffers
  - availability rules
  - conversation state
  - audit logs
- The backend enforces:
  - overlap checks
  - booking locks
  - reschedule rules
  - cancellation rules
  - reminder jobs

Why we chose custom scheduling engine on Postgres
- Most flexible option
- Best if scheduling becomes strategic IP
- Best fit for barber-specific logic
- Lets us support future features like:
  - walk-ins
  - recurring client preferences
  - custom buffers
  - analytics
  - internal admin tooling

Tradeoff we accept
- Highest implementation cost
- More engineering work than Acuity or Calendly
- We must build and maintain:
  - locking
  - overlap detection
  - admin tools
  - reminder workflows
  - operational monitoring

Why we chose WhatsApp Business Platform (Cloud API)
- It is the official Meta API.
- It supports real backend automation and webhooks.
- It fits our architecture better than the WhatsApp Business App.
- It avoids Twilio's extra per-message markup.

Pricing comparison
- WhatsApp Business App
  - cheapest to start
  - manual use only
  - not suitable for the automated receptionist we want
- Twilio
  - easier developer experience
  - extra platform cost
  - Twilio charges its own per-message fee on top of Meta fees
- WhatsApp Business Platform (Cloud API)
  - direct integration with Meta
  - better long-term cost structure
  - more setup and operational responsibility

Rejected or not chosen
- Acuity Scheduling
  - faster and lower risk for MVP
  - rejected because we want full ownership and flexibility
- Calendly
  - acceptable SaaS scheduler
  - rejected because it is less natural for barber-specific service logic
- Google Calendar / Microsoft Graph as main scheduling engine
  - useful for sync and visibility
  - rejected as primary rules engine
- WhatsApp Business App
  - rejected because it is too manual
- Twilio
  - rejected because of extra provider markup
- LLM in core booking flow
  - rejected because it increases operational risk

Final architecture principle
- LLM helps interpret.
- Backend decides.
- Postgres stores truth.
